question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) what guidance his Department has issued to other Executive Departments about managing their budget and overspend risks; and (ii) on what date this guidance was issued.
answer_text: Since March 2022 the following has been issued to Departments in relation to managing their budget and overspend risks.Letter to Permanent Secretaries - 9 March 2022Forecast Outturn Guidance - 25 April 2022Departmental Contingency Planning Letters - 14 April 2022Contingency Position Exercise - 19 May 2022Section 59 letters - 24 August 2022Updated Forecast Outturn Guidance - 15 August 2022Ministerial Budget Update Letter - 11 October 2022The issue of managing Departmental budgets and overspend risks has also been discussed at various Permanent Secretary meetings, regular Finance Director meetings as well as regular stocktakes with departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) how many patients are on the waiting list for surgery as a result of Essure; and (ii) whether he will bring forward a waiting list initiative to ensure women who have had Essure receive timely surgery.
answer_text: Patients who require treatment or surgery associated with Essure procedures are currently included within the general Gynaecology lists with the appropriate priority coding per clinical team, this group of patients is not identified separately on waiting lists.  At present, women who have cancer or suspected cancer are prioritised for surgery and there is therefore a delay in other Gynaecology cases.  Regrettably, demand within gynaecology exceeds the current capacity.There are currently no plans in place to specifically deal with those patients who are waiting on the gynaecology waiting list to have their Essure coil removed as the focus remains on addressing red flags and cancersA key priority for all Trusts is to maximise all elective capacity to ensure that as many patients as possible are seen and treated in an environment that is safe for both patients and staff. As Minister, I will continue to do the best I can to deliver for the public with the resources I have available.Amid ongoing budget uncertainty, I am on record as warning that funding pressures in health may be significant by the second half of 2022/23. The financial situation will undoubtedly be constrained whatever the final budget settlement.My ability to plan strategically is being significantly impaired by the ongoing budgetary uncertainty.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to list the groups and organisations that received project funding from her Department for the Queen's Platinum Jubilee related activities or events.
answer_text: My Department did not introduce any specific funding schemes to mark the Queen’s Platinum Jubilee.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities the number of (i) applicants; and (ii) successful applicants of the Discretionary Support Fund and Short Term Benefit Advance in (a) 2019/20; (b) 2020/21; (c) 2021/22; and (d) this year to date.
answer_text: The Department publishes an annual report which details Discretionary Support performance and expenditure during each financial year.  Further information can be found via the following link https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/welfare-supplementary-payments-discretionary-support-standards-advice-assistance-and-sanctionsThe table below provides details for Short Term Benefit Advance:Short Term Benefit AdvanceApplicants*Successful Applicants**2019-206602612020-21192103*Data is not collected on number of individual applicants but rather on number of applications, one individual may make several applications and receive multiple awards in any financial year. **The number awarded includes claims where an award was offered but was subsequently not accepted by the applicant.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) which roads in Lagan Valley are scheduled for road improvement in the next twelve months, and (ii) the commencement date for each.
answer_text: My Department maintains this information on a DfI Roads Section Office or District Council area basis rather than by Parliamentary / Assembly Constituency.The Lagan Valley constituency is served by the Lisburn and Castlereagh Section Office and Belfast South Section Office within DfI Roads Eastern Division; and Armagh, Banbridge, Craigavon East Section Office and Newry, Mourne and Down East Section Office within DfI Roads Southern Division.Details of works programmes for the 2022/23 financial year will be included in the Department’s Reports to each respective Council which are now being finalised following recent confirmation of the Department’s 2022/23 contingency funding allocation. In due course the Reports will be presented to the Councils, published on the Department’s website and placed in the Assembly library.It should also be noted that delivery of the resurfacing work programmes will be dependent on the successful award of replacement asphalt resurfacing contracts.Should you have any further questions on this please contact DfI Roads Eastern or Southern Divisions at: - dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.ukdfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to list the groups and organisations in the Lagan Valley area that are receiving core or project funding from her Department in this financial year.
answer_text: In this financial year, my Department will provide core or project funding to the following list of groups and organisations in the Lagan Valley area:Atlas Women’s CentreArmagh City, Banbridge and Craigavon Borough Council Lagan Valley Regional ParkLisburn & Castlereagh City Council Lisburn City Church Community TrustStepping Stones - Stepping Stones to Employment 2 projectResurgam TrustAs well as providing funding to Lisburn & Castlereagh City Council to deliver regeneration projects, my Department also provides funding through an annual Community Support Programme.  This facilitates local community development activity and delivery of independent advice services. These funds are distributed by the Council and is likely to include the Lagan Valley Constituency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the (i) amount; and (ii) date, of each budget allocation made to each Executive department since February 2022.
answer_text: In the absence of an Executive it is not possible to agree a Budget for 2022-23 onwards. On 3rd March I provided a written statement to the Assembly which outlined the financial position and provided details of further limited funding allocations in 2021-22.  This statement can be accessed via the following link.(www.finance-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/dfp/Written%20Ministerial%20Statement%20–%202021-22%20Further%20Allocations.pdf)

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the (i) current departmental funding pressures; (ii) reason for these pressures; and (iii) total amount by which these pressures are unfunded.
answer_text: My Department is facing inescapable resource pressures in 2022-23 totalling £188.9m when compared to the opening 2021-22 baseline budget. Reasons for these pressures and total amounts are detailed in the table below.  Energy costs and rising inflation remain extremely volatile and therefore these pressures are subject to change, and are currently under review.  Pressure and reasonAmount £mNI Water – to meet Regulator’s Price Control Determination and increases in energy costs and inflation. 76.2Translink – to meet increased costs to deliver the public transport network, including pay, fuel and energy, combined with forecast reduced fare revenue.78Department – to meet increased costs associated with departmental activities and services, impacted by inflation, increasing energy costs and pay. 34.7Total 188.9Adding to the financial pressures in my Department is the refusal of the DUP to form an Executive which in turn means £420m of funds held at the centre cannot be released. The blocking of the Executive also means a three year budget cannot be agreed which makes it extremely difficult for my Department to plan future investment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what communication he has received from other Executive departments in relation to departmental funding pressures; and what is the total estimated amount of these pressures.
answer_text: The Department of Finance has written to each department requesting information on the funding pressures and are awaiting their individual responses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the (i) current departmental funding pressures; (ii) reason for these pressures; and (iii) total amount by which these pressures are unfunded.
answer_text: Education is facing extensive unavoidable cost pressures and rising service demands associated with delivering statutory and policy obligations.Taking into consideration an indicative baseline of £2,269.6m and only those pressures which are deemed to be inescapable or pre-committed, the Department reported an initial estimated Resource funding gap of £366m for 2022-23, with the gap increasing in subsequent years.  Following a recent review, these pressures are now estimated to be £465m. This covers a range of pressures including teaching and non-teaching pay, the rising costs of funding Special Education Needs, resourcing pressures in schools, School Holiday Food Grant payments, increased energy costs and the Engage II programme. There are also a number of other general Resource education pressures assessed as High Priority or Desirable estimated at £50m for 2022-23, £55m for 2023-24 and £48m for 2024-25.The Department has been advised that the contingency planning envelope for capital spend is £184.2m, which was based on estimates of tails of existing projects, contractual commitments at 4 February 2022, routine programmes and maintenance, and programmes and projects where there is previous Executive agreement.  This includes £34.5m of ring-fenced funding for Fresh Start projects. This is only sufficient to meet the Department’s contractual commitments.In advance of a Final Budget being agreed and in line with Department of Finance guidance, I have made a number of risk-based Resource expenditure planning decisions to ensure continuity of service in the 2022-23 financial year.  This has involved me giving a commitment to fund a number of pressures over and above the indicative baseline for Education, such as: maintaining the Aggregated Schools Budget at the same level as in 2021-22, providing funding to continue the Healthy Happy Minds pilot and the Engage II programme, extending the School Holiday Food Grant payments over the summer period, and the funding of a number of other time sensitive critical areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the total spend to date on Project Stratum in the Lagan Valley constituency.
answer_text: Project Stratum was allocated £165m of public funding, £150m of which was secured under the Confidence and Supply Agreement between the Democratic Unionist Party and the Conservatives, and £15m from the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs. In November 2020 Fibrus Networks was appointed as the contractor to deliver gigabit-capable broadband to over 76,000 premises, using the £165m of public funding, and their own substantial investment of more than £45m. In January 2022, my Department secured additional capital funding of £32m to bring a further 8,469 premises into Project Stratum, including 382 premises in the Lagan Valley Constituency. The inclusion of these additional premises will bring the total number of premises in the Lagan Valley Constituency to benefit from Project Stratum to 4,032.While my Department is not in a position to dis-aggregate funds for Project Stratum to a constituency level, due to the complex nature of the network design and deployment configuration across all constituencies, I can advise that, under Project Stratum in 2021-22, my Department allocated £49.8m of investment as part of Project Stratum, which builds on the £21.1m allocated to the project in 2020-21.Current deployment plans show that over 1,100 premises in the Lagan Valley Constituency had been passed by 11 May 2022, with over 2,000 more expected to be passed in the next year, and continuing phased deployment is expected to pass all 4,032 premises by Spring '25.
